checks.a_analysis: The output document was compared paragraph by paragraph against the source PDF. All text present in the JSON body, including the description of the CSTF and the response process, originates directly from the source. The AI-generated table caption and summary are permitted under the acceptable changes guidelines.

All contact information in the output was verified against the source PDF. This includes the NCDIT Customer Support Center phone number (800-722-3946), the NCEM Watch Center email (NCEOC@ncdps.gov) and phone number (1-800-858-0368), the statewide incident report URL (https://it.nc.gov/report), the CSTF support email (ncisaac@ncsbi.gov), and the DIT threat management email (DITThreatManagement@nc.gov). All entries match the source exactly.

The output contains all sections and pages from the source document. The seven primary headings found in the PDF (including the title) are represented in the JSON structure, and the content from all three pages is fully accounted for.

The table 'Reporting Points of Contact' in the source PDF contains a title row, a sub-header row, and three data rows (one of which has an empty cell in the right column). The JSON output accurately reflects this structure with appropriate colspan and content mappings.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Each section was cross-referenced for completeness. For example, the 'When to Report' section correctly includes all five bullet points even though they were split across pages 1 and 2 in the original PDF. No significant text omissions were identified.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The wording in the JSON output was compared to the source text. Minor variations in punctuation or whitespace (typical of document conversion) do not alter the meaning of the instructions or requirements presented in the document.

checks.a_analysis: The output document represents the text from the three-page source PDF without any unintended duplication. Each paragraph, heading, list item, and table cell occurs exactly once in the sequence corresponding to the source document. Metadata and structural elements like section titles appropriately mirror the document title without redundant repetition of body content.

The heading hierarchy is logical and well-structured. The document title 'Cyber Incident Reporting' serves as the primary heading (Level 1 equivalent in the section structure), followed by several Level 2 headings for major sections (e.g., 'When to Report to the State', 'What to Report', etc.). There are no skipped levels or inconsistent nesting found in the output.

The table on page 2 of the source document is accurately converted. The output correctly identifies the spanning header row ('Reporting Points of Contact'), the column sub-headers ('State Agencies' and 'Local Governments...'), and the subsequent data rows. The empty cell in the bottom-right of the source table is correctly represented as an empty string in the JSON data row, preserving the 2-column structure.

The reading order in the output follows the logical top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow of the source PDF. It begins with the header logos, followed by the title, subtitle, introductory paragraphs, and continues through the sections, lists, and tables in the exact order they appear in the original document.

All bulleted lists in the source document are correctly identified and formatted as unordered lists in the output. The first list, which is visually split between page 1 and page 2 in the PDF, has been correctly merged into a single continuous list of five items in the JSON, which is a preferred structural refinement for accessibility.

The output correctly captures all URLs and email addresses present in the source document as functional links. This includes the 'it.nc.gov/report' URL (appearing twice) and the various email contacts in the table. No fake links were added, and all captured links match the text in the PDF.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully maintains the visual organization and logical flow of the original document. The multi-page PDF structure is correctly converted into a single-page HTML layout with appropriate sectioning. Bulleted lists, headings, and the overall progression of information match the source material. While styling (fonts, spacing) differs, the visual arrangement is consistent with the source's intent.

The 'Reporting Points of Contact' table on page 2 of the PDF is correctly rendered in the HTML. The two-column structure is preserved, and the content within each cell is clear and readable. The header row from the PDF is accurately translated into the HTML table structure.

All meaningful images from the source PDF, including the NCDIT logo, the North Carolina State Seal, and the DPS logo, are present and correctly rendered in the HTML output. No broken images or missing graphical content were identified.

There is no visual duplication of body content in the HTML output. Paragraphs and tables appear once as intended. The presence of section headings in both the Table of Contents and the document body is a standard accessibility feature and does not constitute content duplication.
